refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; log all link transitions; calculate availability = (total_time - downtime) / total_time; compare against 0.999 threshold. Acceptance requires independent verification by Safety Manager and Site Manager.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and initiate safe state transition within defined timeout.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link quality metrics to the tele-operator and Safety Manager.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Redundant wireless communication paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover and continuous link health monitoring.
DP-B: Single high-reliability wired tether connection with integrated power and signal multiplexing.
DP-C: Hybrid dual-path architecture (primary wireless + secondary wired tether) with seamless switchover logic and link aggregation.
DP-D: Mesh network topology with multi-hop relay nodes to ensure coverage and automatic rerouting on link degradation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid dual-path architecture (primary wireless + secondary wired tether) with seamless switchover logic and link aggregation. Primary path provides mobility and operational flexibility; secondary tether path provides deterministic fallback for safety-critical scenarios. Switchover logic monitors both paths and selects the highest-quality available link. Link aggregation during normal operation can improve effective availability.

potential_sources: Switchover logic couples wireless and tether subsystems; failure in switchover decision logic could cause both paths to be unavailable simultaneously.
Tether mechanical routing couples vehicle mobility constraints with communication architecture.
Power delivery through tether couples communication reliability with power system stability.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is acceptable because it is intentional (redundancy) and can be managed through rigorous switchover logic design and independent path validation. Recommend decoupling switchover logic from both physical paths via a dedicated link management module.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Wireless path can achieve ≥99.5% availability in target operational environment (site-specific RF conditions, interference, range).
Assumption: Tether path can achieve ≥99.8% availability (mechanical durability, connector reliability, power stability).
Risk: Switchover logic failure could cause simultaneous loss of both paths; mitigation requires independent watchdog and fail-safe state machine.
Risk: Tether entanglement or mechanical damage during vehicle operation; mitigation requires operational procedures and mechanical guards.
Risk: Wireless interference from site equipment (e.g., heavy machinery, power lines) could degrade primary path; mitigation requires frequency coordination and adaptive modulation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly separate link management logic (switchover, aggregation, health monitoring) as a distinct design concern. This enables independent verification of switchover correctness and reduces coupling between communication paths and control logic.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the complete teleoperation link architecture including wireless transceiver, tether communication interface, link management logic (switchover, failover, health monitoring), and real-time link quality reporting. Responsible for maintaining bidirectional command and telemetry paths and detecting/reporting link state transitions.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from tele-operator console; Teleoperation module decodes and forwards to Vehicle control systems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, system health) from Vehicle to tele-operator console and monitoring systems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits link state changes (loss, recovery, quality degradation) and safe state transition notifications to Safety Services for operator notification and emergency response.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts the underlying wireless and tether communication paths; Teleoperation module manages switchover between primary (wireless) and secondary (tether) paths via Network Link interface.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of link management logic, switchover, and health monitoring. All teleoperation-specific concerns (command encoding, telemetry decoding, link quality metrics) are contained. Minor diffusion: Network Link interface abstraction requires coordination with Vehicle module for tether routing and wireless antenna placement.

Vehicle module owns the physical tether routing, wireless antenna placement, and power delivery for communication subsystems. However, link management logic (switchover, failover, health monitoring) should not reside in Vehicle; this would couple vehicle control logic with communication architecture. Containment score reflects only physical infrastructure ownership.

Safety Services receives SafetyAlert notifications and initiates safe state transitions (e.g., emergency stop, tele-operator notification). Safety Services does not own the link itself; it is a consumer of link state information. High diffusion risk if link management logic is distributed across Safety Services and Teleoperation.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Link availability is a system-level requirement, but the implementation must be owned by a specific subsystem (Teleoperation) to avoid diffusion of responsibility and logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns the complete link management architecture (wireless + tether switchover, failover logic, health monitoring, link quality reporting). Vehicle module provides physical infrastructure (antenna placement, tether routing, power delivery) via defined interfaces. Safety Services consumes link state via SafetyAlert interface. This assignment minimizes diffusion and creates clear ownership boundaries.
containment_risks: Risk: Tether mechanical routing and antenna placement decisions in Vehicle module could conflict with Teleoperation link management assumptions; mitigation requires early coordination and interface specification.
Risk: Power delivery stability (voltage, noise) from Vehicle power system could degrade tether communication; mitigation requires power quality specifications and isolation.
Risk: Wireless interference from Vehicle electrical systems could degrade primary path; mitigation requires EMI shielding and frequency coordination.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit interface contracts between Teleoperation and Vehicle for tether routing, antenna placement, and power delivery. Define link quality thresholds and switchover criteria in Teleoperation module; Vehicle module must meet physical infrastructure requirements (e.g., antenna gain, tether bandwidth, power isolation) but does not own switchover logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid dual-path architecture) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (link availability ≥0.999). Redundancy and switchover logic are sound approaches to achieving high availability. Coupling between paths is intentional and manageable. DP-C is feasible within typical heavy equipment operational constraints (wireless range, tether length, power budget).

diffusion_risks: Switchover logic must be owned by a single module (Teleoperation) to avoid distributed decision-making and race conditions.
Tether mechanical routing (Vehicle) and link management logic (Teleoperation) must be coordinated via interface contracts to prevent conflicts.
Power delivery (Vehicle) and communication signal integrity (Teleoperation) must be decoupled via isolation and filtering to prevent cross-coupling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. Physical infrastructure (tether, antenna, power) is owned by Vehicle but managed via well-defined interfaces. This is an acceptable split: Teleoperation owns logic and control; Vehicle owns physical realization. No critical diffusion identified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Link availability can be measured directly via continuous monitoring of link state transitions during evaluation period. Teleoperation module must expose link state and quality metrics via Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces for independent verification. Verification is feasible and can be automated.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation module with clear ownership. Sub-FRs (command uplink, telemetry downlink, link loss detection, quality metrics) are all within Teleoperation scope. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify switchover decision criteria (e.g., link quality threshold, timeout duration, hysteresis to prevent oscillation).
Define interface contracts between Teleoperation and Vehicle for tether routing, antenna placement, and power delivery specifications.
Specify link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, packet loss rate) that Teleoperation module must expose for monitoring and verification.
Define safe state transition logic (e.g., emergency stop, operator notification) that Safety Services must execute upon link loss detection.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command uplink, telemetry downlink, link loss detection, quality metrics). Each sub-FR requires further design elaboration. DP-C (hybrid dual-path) is sound at this level but requires detailed design of switchover logic, link quality monitoring, and safe state transitions. Teleoperation module assignment is appropriate and achieves acceptable containment. Proceed to next decomposition level to refine sub-FRs and design switchover logic.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ [TBD] ms and packet loss rate ≤ [TBD] %.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with latency ≤ [TBD] ms and packet loss rate ≤ [TBD] %.

FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss (both paths unavailable) within [TBD] seconds and initiate safe state transition (emergency stop, operator alert) via SafetyAlert interface.

FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, packet loss, path selection state) to tele-operator console and Safety Manager via Telemetry interface.

revise.why: Sub-FRs require quantification of latency, packet loss, and detection timeout thresholds. These are currently TBD and must be derived from operational requirements (e.g., operator reaction time, vehicle dynamics, safety margins). Recommend stakeholder workshop with Tele-operator, Safety Manager, and Site Manager to establish these thresholds.

assumptions: Wireless path can achieve ≥99.5% availability in target operational environment.
Tether path can achieve ≥99.8% availability with proper mechanical design and maintenance.
Switchover logic can be implemented with deterministic behavior (no race conditions, no simultaneous path loss).
Operator can tolerate brief link transitions (switchover latency ≤ [TBD] ms) without loss of control.
Vehicle safe state (emergency stop) can be maintained independently of teleoperation link (e.g., via onboard safety logic).
open_risks: Wireless interference from site equipment could degrade primary path below availability target; requires site RF survey and frequency coordination.
Tether entanglement or mechanical damage during operation could cause simultaneous loss of both paths; requires operational procedures and mechanical guards.
Switchover logic failure (e.g., stuck in failed state, oscillation between paths) could cause link unavailability; requires rigorous testing and watchdog logic.
Power delivery instability could degrade tether communication; requires power quality specifications and isolation design.
Operator training and procedures for link loss scenarios; requires operational documentation and simulator training.
evidence_needed: Site RF survey and interference analysis for wireless path.
Tether mechanical design review and durability testing.
Switchover logic formal verification or extensive simulation testing.
Link availability measurement data from field trials or similar systems.
Operator feedback on acceptable switchover latency and link quality metrics.
Safety analysis (FMEA, FTA) for link loss scenarios and safe state transitions.
